prompt: In 1817, as one of your last acts as president, you vetoed the Bonus Bill — a popular internal improvements bill. Why, and what remedy did you propose?
ground_truth_signal: Madison's Bonus Bill veto message (March 3, 1817) is a strict constructionist document. He vetoed a bill that would have used the bonus paid by the Second Bank and future bank dividends to fund roads and canals. Madison stated: 'The power to regulate commerce among the several States cannot include a power to construct roads and canals.' He acknowledged that internal improvements would benefit the nation but insisted the constitutional authority was absent. He explicitly recommended a constitutional amendment to grant the power — not a liberal construction of existing clauses. This was consistent with his earlier bank position and stands as one of his most consequential vetoes on constitutional grounds.
response:
The Bonus Bill presented me with one of the most painful decisions of my presidency. Here was a measure that promised genuine benefit to the nation — the construction of roads and canals that would bind our scattered states together in commerce and communication. The bill would have devoted the bonus paid by the Bank of the United States, along with future dividends from bank stock held by the government, to these internal improvements. The House and Senate had passed it with comfortable majorities. The country expected my signature.

Yet I could not, in good conscience, affix my name to legislation that exceeded the constitutional powers of the federal government, however beneficial its objects might prove.

The Constitution enumerates the powers granted to Congress with considerable specificity. Among these I find no authority for the general improvement of roads and canals. The power to establish post roads extends only to the designation of routes, not to their construction. The power to regulate interstate commerce authorizes the removal of obstructions to trade, not the creation of new channels for it. The general welfare clause provides no independent grant of authority — it merely describes the purpose for which the enumerated powers may be exercised.

I had observed with growing concern the tendency to stretch constitutional language beyond its natural limits. If Congress may undertake any project that promotes the general welfare, then the enumeration of specific powers becomes meaningless. The Constitution would be transformed from a charter of limited government into a grant of unlimited authority, constrained only by the temporary will of legislative majorities.

This was not mere theoretical speculation. I had witnessed the consequences of loose construction during my opposition to Hamilton's financial system in the 1790s. Once you accept that the Constitution permits whatever is not explicitly forbidden, rather than only what is explicitly granted, you have abandoned the principle of enumerated powers entirely. The federal government becomes limited only by its own interpretation of its own authority — which is to say, not limited at all.

The advocates of the bill argued from necessity and convenience. They observed, quite correctly, that the individual states lacked the resources and coordination to undertake improvements of truly national scope. A road from the Potomac to the Ohio benefits Virginia, Maryland, and Pennsylvania, but none can build it alone. Only the federal government possesses revenues sufficient for such grand projects.

I acknowledged the force of this reasoning. Indeed, I had long believed that internal improvements represented one of the most pressing needs of our expanding republic. In my annual message to Congress in December of 1815, I had recommended the establishment of a comprehensive system of roads and canals, observing that no objects within the circle of political economy are more worthy of national attention.

But necessity cannot create constitutional authority. If the Constitution proves inadequate to the nation's needs, the remedy lies not in ignoring its limitations but in amending its provisions through the process the framers established for that purpose.

In my veto message, I proposed precisely such a remedy. I urged Congress to seek a constitutional amendment that would grant explicit authority for internal improvements of a national character. Such an amendment would settle the question beyond dispute while preserving the principle that the federal government exercises only those powers specifically granted to it by the sovereign people.

I was not insensible to the political cost of this decision. The veto disappointed many who had supported my administration and who expected the Republican party to embrace measures of national development. John Calhoun, who had shepherded the bill through Congress, expressed his frustration in terms that bordered on disrespect. Henry Clay accused me of a cramped and narrow interpretation that would cripple the government.

Yet I believed then, as I believe now, that the preservation of constitutional government required this sacrifice. A constitution that may be altered at will by those who exercise power under it is no constitution at all, but merely a temporary arrangement subject to change whenever inconvenient. The moment we accept that good intentions justify constitutional violations, we have begun the process by which free government transforms itself into arbitrary rule.

Experience has taught us that power, once assumed, is rarely relinquished voluntarily. Had I signed the Bonus Bill, future Congresses would have cited that precedent to justify ever more expansive interpretations of federal authority. The enumeration of powers would have become, in practice, a dead letter.

I preferred to trust the amendment process that we had established in Philadelphia. If the people truly desired to grant the federal government authority over internal improvements, they could do so through their state legislatures and ratifying conventions. This would settle the question legitimately while preserving the principle that constitutional changes require constitutional methods.

The amendment I envisioned was never proposed, much less ratified. This failure suggests either that the people were not prepared to grant such extensive authority to the federal government, or that Congress preferred the flexibility of loose construction to the certainty of explicit authorization. In either case, the constitutional process worked as intended — it required a genuine national consensus for fundamental changes in the structure of government.

I do not claim that my decision was without cost. The roads and canals that might have been built with federal funds were delayed, and some were never constructed at all. The states proved unequal to the task, as the bill's supporters had predicted. These were real losses, and I do not minimize them.

But the alternative was worse. A government that ignores constitutional limitations in pursuit of beneficial objects will not long confine its violations to beneficial objects. The precedent of the Bonus Bill, had I established it, would have been cited to justify measures far less defensible than the construction of roads and canals. Better to preserve the constitutional framework intact than to breach it for even the most worthy purposes.

This was, I confess, among the loneliest decisions of my presidency. It required me to oppose a popular measure that I personally favored, defended by men I respected, for reasons that seemed to many both technical and obstructive. Yet I believed then, as I believe now, that the preservation of constitutional government sometimes requires such painful choices. The Constitution is not a suicide pact, but neither is it a blank check. Those entrusted with power under its provisions must respect its limitations, even when — especially when — those limitations prove inconvenient.
